subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Making an Offer to Settle — Requirements Under r.49.02
fact_pattern: Delacroix Manufacturing Ltd. is the defendant in a breach of supply contract action. Examinations for discovery have been completed. Delacroix's counsel telephones plaintiff's counsel and verbally proposes settling the entire action for $95,000. Plaintiff's counsel says she will consider it. Two weeks later, Delacroix's counsel sends a signed written document to plaintiff's counsel by email, again proposing settlement for $95,000 and specifying that the offer is open until 30 days before trial. The plaintiff rejects both the telephone proposal and the written document. The trial proceeds, and the plaintiff recovers only $80,000. Delacroix seeks cost consequences under r.49.10(1).
call_of_question: Which of Delacroix's proposals, if either, qualifies as an offer to settle capable of attracting cost consequences under r.49.10?

options.A: Both the telephone proposal and the written document qualify as offers to settle under r.49.02, because both communicated a genuine willingness to settle for $95,000.
options.B: Neither proposal qualifies because an offer to settle must be filed with the court and assigned a court file number to be effective under r.49.02.
options.C: Only the written document qualifies; under r.49.02, an offer to settle must be in writing and served on the opposing party to be effective — the oral telephone proposal does not satisfy these requirements.
options.D: Neither proposal qualifies because r.49.10 cost consequences are only available for offers made before examinations for discovery have been completed.

explanation: Under r.49.02(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a party may make an offer to settle at any time. Under r.49.02(2), the offer must be in writing. Under r.49.02(3), the offer is effective when it is served on the other party. The oral telephone proposal does not satisfy the writing requirement and therefore cannot attract cost consequences under r.49.10. The written document signed by counsel and sent to plaintiff's counsel satisfies all three requirements — it is in writing, it is signed, and it was served (by email, which is an accepted method of service on a represented party). The written offer was made after discoveries, which is permissible — r.49.02 imposes no discovery deadline. The written offer at $95,000 was not beaten by the plaintiff's $80,000 recovery, so r.49.10(1) applies to that offer.
why_A_wrong: The oral telephone proposal does not qualify as an offer to settle under r.49.02. The rule requires the offer to be in writing; verbal settlement discussions, however genuine, cannot attract cost consequences under r.49.10. (Pragmatic Bluff — treating genuine intent as sufficient without meeting the formal requirements)
why_B_wrong: Offers to settle are not filed with the court and do not require a court file number. They are served on the opposing party and are effective upon service under r.49.02(3). Filing with the court is not a requirement under r.49. (Wrong Forum/Procedure Trap — inventing a filing requirement that does not exist)
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Only the written, signed, and served document qualifies under r.49.02; the oral telephone proposal does not meet the writing requirement.
why_D_wrong: Rule 49.02(1) permits an offer to settle to be made at any time during the proceeding. There is no rule limiting r.49.10 cost consequences to offers made before discoveries have been completed. Offers made after discovery are equally capable of attracting cost consequences. (Wrong Timeline Trap — inventing a pre-discovery deadline that does not exist in the Rules)

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Making an Offer to Settle — Requirements Under r.49.02
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.49.02

oral proposal does not qualify for r.49.10
effective upon service
offer may be made at any time including after discoveries

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Withdrawal and Expiry of Offers to Settle — r.49.04
fact_pattern: The plaintiff serves a written offer to settle on the defendant on April 10, 2026. The offer does not state a specific expiry date. On April 19, 2026 — nine days after service — the defendant serves a written acceptance of the offer. The plaintiff argues the offer had already lapsed because more than a week has passed.
call_of_question: Under r.49.04 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, when does an offer to settle that does not specify an expiry date expire?

options.A: An offer expires automatically 7 days after service if no expiry date is stated.
options.B: An offer does not expire if no expiry date is stated; it remains open for acceptance indefinitely until withdrawn.
options.C: An offer expires automatically 10 days after service if no expiry date is stated.
options.D: An offer without an expiry date expires at the commencement of the trial.

explanation: Under r.49.04 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), an offer to settle that does not specify an expiry date may be accepted at any time before the commencement of the hearing (trial). The offer does not automatically expire after 7 days or 10 days merely because no expiry date is stated. In this scenario, the defendant's acceptance on April 19 — nine days after service and before the commencement of trial — is a valid acceptance of the plaintiff's offer. The plaintiff is incorrect that the offer lapsed.
why_A_wrong: There is no automatic 7-day expiry rule for an offer that does not specify an expiry date. Rule 49.04 permits acceptance at any time before the commencement of the hearing.
why_B_wrong: Offers do have a temporal limit even when no expiry date is specified. Under r.49.04, an offer without an expiry date may only be accepted before the commencement of the hearing (trial).
why_C_wrong: There is no automatic 10-day expiry rule for an offer without a stated expiry date. Rule 49.04 specifies that such offers may be accepted until the commencement of the hearing.
why_D_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under r.49.04, an offer without a stated expiry date may be accepted at any time before the commencement of the hearing (trial).

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Withdrawal and Expiry of Offers to Settle — r.49.04
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.49.04

r.49.04 — expiry of offers
timing of acceptance of offer to settle

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Cost Consequences — Defendant's Offer Better Than Plaintiff's Recovery
fact_pattern: The defendant serves a written offer to settle for $80,000 on the plaintiff. The plaintiff rejects the offer and proceeds to trial. At trial, the plaintiff is awarded judgment for $70,000 — less than the defendant's offer. The defendant seeks enhanced cost consequences.
call_of_question: Under r.49.10 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what are the cost consequences where the plaintiff fails to obtain a judgment more favourable than the defendant's offer to settle?

options.A: The plaintiff is entitled to full solicitor-client costs for the entire action because the plaintiff succeeded at trial.
options.B: There are no cost consequences because the plaintiff obtained a judgment; the plaintiff's entitlement to partial indemnity costs for the entire action is unaffected.
options.C: Under r.49.10(1), the plaintiff is entitled to partial indemnity costs to the date of the offer, but the defendant is entitled to partial indemnity costs from the date the offer was made.
options.D: Under r.49.10(1), the plaintiff is entitled to partial indemnity costs to the date the offer was made, but the defendant is entitled to substantial indemnity costs from that date.

explanation: Under r.49.10(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), where the defendant makes an offer to settle that is not accepted, and the plaintiff obtains a judgment that is no more favourable than the terms of the offer, the plaintiff is entitled to partial indemnity costs to the date the offer was made. However, the defendant is entitled to partial indemnity costs from the date the offer was made. This split costs award is designed to incentivise the acceptance of reasonable offers: the plaintiff recovers normal costs up to the offer date but must pay the defendant's partial indemnity costs after that date. Note: under the current Rules, the defendant is entitled to partial indemnity costs (not substantial indemnity costs) unless the court orders otherwise.
why_A_wrong: The plaintiff is not entitled to solicitor-client costs simply for succeeding at trial. Moreover, the plaintiff here obtained a judgment less favourable than the defendant's offer, which triggers the adverse cost consequence under r.49.10(1).
why_B_wrong: There are significant cost consequences where the plaintiff fails to beat the defendant's offer. Rule 49.10(1) provides the defendant with partial indemnity costs from the date of the offer.
why_C_wrong: Under r.49.10(1), it is the defendant who is entitled to partial indemnity costs from the date of the offer. The plaintiff is entitled to partial indemnity costs only up to that date.
why_D_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 49.10(1) gives the plaintiff partial indemnity costs to the date of the defendant's offer, and the defendant partial indemnity costs from that date forward.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Cost Consequences — Defendant's Offer Better Than Plaintiff's Recovery
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.49.10(1)

tested_concepts: r.49.10(1) — defendant's offer not beaten by plaintiff's recovery
plaintiff gets partial indemnity to date of offer
defendant gets partial indemnity from date of offer

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Cost Consequences — Plaintiff's Offer Beaten by Plaintiff's Recovery
fact_pattern: The plaintiff served an offer to settle for $150,000. The defendant rejected the offer. At trial, the plaintiff was awarded $175,000 — more than the plaintiff's offer. The plaintiff seeks enhanced costs from the date of the offer.
call_of_question: Under r.49.10(2) of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what are the cost consequences where the plaintiff obtains a judgment more favourable than their own offer to settle?

options.A: The plaintiff is entitled to partial indemnity costs for the entire action from the start to the end of trial.
options.B: The plaintiff is entitled to partial indemnity costs to the date the offer was made, and substantial indemnity costs from the date of the offer to the end of trial.
options.C: The plaintiff is entitled to solicitor-client costs for the entire action because the defendant unreasonably refused the offer.
options.D: There are no additional cost consequences beyond the ordinary partial indemnity costs award; the plaintiff's offer to settle has no relevance to costs.

explanation: Under r.49.10(2) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), where the plaintiff makes an offer to settle that is not accepted, and the plaintiff obtains a judgment as favourable as or more favourable than the terms of the offer, the plaintiff is entitled to partial indemnity costs to the date the offer was made, and substantial indemnity costs from the date of the offer to the end of trial. This enhanced cost consequence — the shift from partial to substantial indemnity — is the financial incentive for defendants to accept reasonable plaintiff offers. Substantial indemnity costs are approximately 1.5 times partial indemnity costs.
why_A_wrong: The plaintiff does not receive only partial indemnity costs for the entire action. Rule 49.10(2) provides for a split: partial indemnity to the date of offer and substantial indemnity after the offer date.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 49.10(2) entitles the plaintiff to partial indemnity costs to the date of the offer and substantial indemnity costs from that date, where the plaintiff beats their own offer.
why_C_wrong: Solicitor-client costs (full indemnity) are not automatically awarded where a plaintiff beats their offer. Rule 49.10(2) provides substantial indemnity costs from the date of the offer, not solicitor-client costs.
why_D_wrong: A plaintiff's offer to settle is highly relevant to costs. Rule 49.10(2) creates a significant cost shift — from partial to substantial indemnity — when the plaintiff beats their own offer.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Cost Consequences — Plaintiff's Offer Beaten by Plaintiff's Recovery
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.49.10(2)

tested_concepts: r.49.10(2) — plaintiff beats their own offer
partial indemnity to date of offer then substantial indemnity
substantial indemnity as incentive to accept

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: During the course of a personal injury action, the plaintiff makes three separate offers to settle: one for $200,000 (declined), one for $175,000 (declined), and one for $150,000 (declined). The defendant also makes one offer for $100,000 (declined). At trial, the plaintiff is awarded $160,000. Both parties seek cost consequences based on their respective offers.
call_of_question: Under r.49.13 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, how does the court determine which offer governs the cost consequences where multiple offers have been made?

options.A: The court applies the cost consequences based on the plaintiff's first offer only; subsequent offers supersede prior offers and are treated as withdrawals.
options.B: The court applies cost consequences based on the most recent offer made before the close of trial.
options.C: Under r.49.13, the court has discretion to take into account any or all of the offers made in determining the appropriate cost consequences; the court is not restricted to a single offer.
options.D: The plaintiff's most recent offer ($150,000) governs exclusively because it is the only offer that was beaten by the trial award.

explanation: Under r.49.13 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), despite r.49.10, the court, in exercising its discretion to award costs, may take into account any offer to settle made in writing, the date the offer was made, the terms of the offer, and the reasonableness of the parties' conduct in relation to the offer. Rule 49.13 gives the court broad discretion to consider the totality of the settlement history and all offers made by any party when determining the appropriate cost award. The court is not strictly bound to apply the automatic consequences of r.49.10 where multiple offers complicate the analysis; it can craft a just costs order considering all the circumstances.
why_A_wrong: Rule 49.13 does not limit the court to considering only the first offer. The court has discretion to consider all offers made; a subsequent offer does not automatically treat prior offers as withdrawn for cost purposes.
why_B_wrong: The court does not automatically apply cost consequences based on the most recent offer. Rule 49.13 gives the court discretion to consider any and all of the offers made and all relevant circumstances.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 49.13 gives the court discretion to consider any or all of the offers made when determining costs, not restricting it to a single offer or automatic application of r.49.10.
why_D_wrong: Rule 49.13 does not require the court to apply consequences based exclusively on the plaintiff's $150,000 offer. The court may consider all three plaintiff offers, the defendant's offer, and all surrounding circumstances.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Multiple Offers to Settle — r.49.13
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.49.13

tested_concepts: r.49.13 — court discretion over multiple offers
court not restricted to single offer for cost consequences
r.49.13 as override of automatic r.49.10 consequences

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Withdrawal of an Offer Before Acceptance — r.49.04
fact_pattern: The plaintiff served a written offer to settle for $120,000 on October 1. On October 15, before the defendant responded, the plaintiff's counsel served a written notice of withdrawal of the offer. On October 17, the defendant's counsel served a written acceptance of the $120,000 offer.
call_of_question: Under r.49.04 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, is the defendant's acceptance of October 17 effective to create a binding settlement?

options.A: Yes — once an offer is served, it cannot be withdrawn unilaterally; the defendant's acceptance is binding and a settlement has been concluded.
options.B: Yes — an offer to settle can only be withdrawn after the offeree has had a reasonable time to consider it; 14 days is not a reasonable time, so the withdrawal is ineffective.
options.C: No — under r.49.04(1), an offer to settle may be withdrawn at any time before it is accepted, by serving written notice of withdrawal on the other party; a withdrawal served before acceptance terminates the offer.
options.D: No — the offer is deemed accepted because the defendant accepted within 30 days of service of the original offer.

explanation: Under r.49.04(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), an offer to settle may be withdrawn at any time before it is accepted, by serving a written notice of withdrawal on the party to whom the offer was made. The offer was made on October 1 and the written notice of withdrawal was served on October 15 — before any acceptance by the defendant. The defendant's purported acceptance on October 17 was therefore made after the offer had already been validly withdrawn, and no binding settlement was created. There is no minimum period during which an offeror must hold an offer open.
why_A_wrong: Rule 49.04(1) expressly provides that an offer may be withdrawn at any time before acceptance by serving written notice of withdrawal. An offer to settle is not irrevocable once served.
why_B_wrong: There is no 'reasonable time' minimum that must elapse before an offer may be withdrawn under r.49.04. The withdrawal right is exercisable at any time before acceptance, without any waiting period.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 49.04(1) permits withdrawal at any time before acceptance by written notice; the plaintiff's withdrawal on October 15 was effective and the October 17 acceptance has no legal effect.
why_D_wrong: There is no 30-day deemed acceptance rule. An offer may be withdrawn at any time before the offeree serves an acceptance; the 30-day period is not a concept found in r.49.04.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Withdrawal of an Offer Before Acceptance — r.49.04
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.49.04(1)

tested_concepts: offer may be withdrawn at any time before acceptance — r.49.04(1)
withdrawal by written notice effective before acceptance
no minimum holding period

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Cost Consequences — Offer Not Accepted; Plaintiff's Recovery Exceeds Defendant's Offer
fact_pattern: In a product liability action, the defendant served a written offer to settle for $200,000 two months before trial. The plaintiff rejected the offer and proceeded to trial. The plaintiff was awarded $250,000 — more than the defendant's offer. The defendant argues that because the judgment exceeded its own offer, there should be no cost consequences against the plaintiff, as the plaintiff 'beat' the defendant's offer.
call_of_question: Under r.49.10 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, does the cost consequences provision apply where the plaintiff obtains a judgment more favourable than the defendant's offer?

options.A: Yes — r.49.10(1) still applies, and the plaintiff must pay the defendant's partial indemnity costs from the date of the offer, regardless of the outcome.
options.B: No — r.49.10(1) applies only where the plaintiff's recovery is no more favourable than the defendant's offer; where the plaintiff's recovery exceeds the defendant's offer, the defendant's offer is not triggered and the ordinary costs follow the event principle applies.
options.C: Yes — r.49.10(1) applies to any rejected offer, and the plaintiff must always pay substantial indemnity costs from the date the defendant made any offer, regardless of the trial outcome.
options.D: No — where the plaintiff's recovery exceeds any offer made by either party, costs are fixed at zero because all offers failed.

explanation: Under r.49.10(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the defendant's cost-shifting consequence is triggered only where the plaintiff obtains a judgment that is no more favourable than the terms of the defendant's offer. In other words, the plaintiff must fail to beat the defendant's offer for r.49.10(1) to apply. Where, as here, the plaintiff's recovery of $250,000 exceeds the defendant's offer of $200,000, the plaintiff has obtained a result more favourable than the defendant's offer. Rule 49.10(1) is not triggered. The ordinary costs-follow-the-event rule applies, and the plaintiff is entitled to its costs as the successful party. The defendant's offer created no adverse cost consequence for the plaintiff.
why_A_wrong: Rule 49.10(1) is not triggered where the plaintiff beats the defendant's offer. The cost-shifting provision only applies where the plaintiff's recovery is no more favourable than the defendant's offer.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 49.10(1) requires that the plaintiff's recovery be no more favourable than the defendant's offer to trigger the cost-shifting consequences. Where the plaintiff beats the defendant's offer, ordinary costs apply.
why_C_wrong: Rule 49.10(1) does not apply to every rejected defendant's offer. The triggering condition is that the plaintiff's recovery does not exceed the offer. Substantial indemnity is not automatically imposed on the plaintiff simply because the defendant made an offer.
why_D_wrong: Where the plaintiff beats the defendant's offer at trial and succeeds, costs do not fall to zero. The successful plaintiff is entitled to ordinary costs on the costs-follow-the-event principle; no offer-to-settle cost consequence arises.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Cost Consequences — Offer Not Accepted; Plaintiff's Recovery Exceeds Defendant's Offer
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.49.10(1)

tested_concepts: r.49.10(1) — triggered only if plaintiff fails to beat defendant's offer
where plaintiff beats offer — no r.49.10(1) consequence
ordinary costs-follow-the-event where offer not triggered

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Accepting an Offer — Mechanics and Binding Effect — r.49.07
fact_pattern: The defendant serves a written offer to settle the action for $95,000. The plaintiff's counsel serves written acceptance of the offer within the time specified. The defendant then has a change of heart and argues that the acceptance does not bind it because the parties have not yet signed a formal settlement agreement.
call_of_question: Under r.49.07 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, when does acceptance of an offer to settle become binding, and is a subsequent formal settlement agreement required?

options.A: Acceptance of an offer to settle does not create a binding agreement until the parties execute a formal written settlement agreement; without such a document, either party may resile.
options.B: Under r.49.07, acceptance of an offer to settle is binding on the parties when the acceptance is served on the party who made the offer; no additional formal settlement agreement is needed to make the acceptance binding.
options.C: Acceptance of an offer to settle is only binding if the accepting party files a consent to judgment with the court within 10 days of the acceptance.
options.D: Acceptance of an offer to settle creates only an agreement to negotiate; the parties must then proceed to mediation before the settlement becomes binding.

explanation: Under r.49.07(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), an acceptance of an offer to settle is binding on the accepting party from the time it is served on the party who made the offer. The acceptance constitutes a binding contract of settlement at the moment of service; no subsequent formal settlement agreement, consent to judgment, or mediation is required to make it enforceable. The defendant in the scenario cannot resile from the accepted offer. Where necessary, the plaintiff can move to enforce the settlement agreement or obtain a consent judgment on the terms of the accepted offer.
why_A_wrong: A formal written settlement agreement is not required for an accepted offer to be binding. Rule 49.07 expressly provides that acceptance is binding upon service; the parties need not execute any further documentation.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under r.49.07, acceptance is binding from the moment it is served on the offeror; no formal settlement agreement is needed.
why_C_wrong: There is no requirement to file a consent to judgment within 10 days of acceptance. The acceptance itself, upon service, creates a binding obligation under r.49.07.
why_D_wrong: Acceptance of an offer to settle creates an immediately binding settlement contract, not merely an agreement to negotiate. Mediation is not a step required by r.49.07 before the settlement becomes enforceable.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Accepting an Offer — Mechanics and Binding Effect — r.49.07
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.49.07(1)

tested_concepts: r.49.07 — acceptance binding upon service
no formal settlement agreement required
defendant cannot resile after binding acceptance

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Offer Timing — When Offer Must Be Made to Trigger r.49.10
fact_pattern: During the course of a trial, after one day of evidence has been heard, the defendant serves an offer to settle for $60,000. At the conclusion of the three-day trial, the plaintiff is awarded $45,000 — less than the defendant's offer. The defendant seeks cost consequences under r.49.10(1) from the date of the offer. The plaintiff argues the offer was made after the trial had commenced and cannot attract the r.49.10 cost consequences.
call_of_question: Under r.49.10(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure, may an offer to settle made after trial has commenced trigger cost consequences if the plaintiff fails to beat it?

options.A: An offer to settle may only trigger r.49.10 cost consequences if it was made before the action was set down for trial; offers made closer to or during trial have no cost consequences.
options.B: Under r.49.10, an offer to settle may be made at any time, including during trial, and may trigger cost consequences if the plaintiff fails to obtain a result more favourable than the offer; the rule does not prohibit offers made after trial has commenced.
options.C: An offer made during trial is automatically inadmissible as evidence of settlement discussions and has no cost consequences under r.49.10.
options.D: Offers made after trial has commenced are treated as counteroffers to the proceedings and automatically settle the action on the terms of the offer.

explanation: Under r.49.02(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a party may make an offer to settle at any time during the proceeding. Rule 49.10 applies to any written offer to settle that was not accepted and that was made at any time in the proceedings. There is no restriction limiting r.49.10 cost consequences to offers made before trial commenced. An offer made during trial — even on day one of a three-day trial — may trigger r.49.10 consequences if the result at trial does not beat the offer. The court may consider the timing of the offer in exercising its overall costs discretion under r.49.13, but the offer is valid and capable of triggering cost consequences under r.49.10.
why_A_wrong: There is no rule requiring offers to be made before the action is set down for trial to attract r.49.10 consequences. Rule 49.02(1) permits offers at any time, and r.49.10 applies accordingly.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under r.49.02 and r.49.10, an offer made at any time — including during trial — may trigger cost consequences if the plaintiff fails to beat the offer.
why_C_wrong: The rule against admission of settlement discussions (without prejudice privilege) concerns admissibility of settlement communications in evidence to prove liability — it does not prevent cost consequences for failing to accept a properly served offer to settle under r.49.
why_D_wrong: An offer made during trial does not automatically settle the action. It is simply an offer that may be accepted or rejected; if rejected and the trial result does not beat the offer, cost consequences under r.49.10 follow.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Offer Timing — When Offer Must Be Made to Trigger r.49.10
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.49.02(1); r.49.10

tested_concepts: r.49.02 — offer may be made at any time
r.49.10 cost consequences not limited to pre-trial offers
timing during trial is discretionary factor under r.49.13

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: The defendant serves a written offer to settle for $50,000. Rather than accepting, the plaintiff serves a written offer to settle for $75,000. The plaintiff's offer is rejected. The plaintiff then seeks to accept the defendant's original $50,000 offer. The defendant argues that the plaintiff's $75,000 offer constituted a counteroffer that terminated the defendant's original offer.
call_of_question: Under r.49 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, does the plaintiff's counter-offer terminate the defendant's original offer to settle?

options.A: Under the Rules of Civil Procedure, a counteroffer does not affect an existing offer; both offers remain open simultaneously until they expire under r.49.04.
options.B: Under the general law of contract as applied to offers to settle under r.49, making a counteroffer does not automatically terminate the original offer because r.49 creates a distinct regime for settlement offers; a party may still accept the original offer unless it has been withdrawn by the offeror.
options.C: Under general contract law principles, making a counteroffer constitutes a rejection of the original offer, which terminates the original offer; the plaintiff cannot accept the defendant's $50,000 offer after making a counteroffer.
options.D: A counteroffer under r.49 must be in the same amount as the original offer to be effective; the plaintiff's $75,000 offer does not qualify as a counteroffer.

explanation: Under Rule 49 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194) creates a specific statutory regime for offers to settle that modifies ordinary contract law principles. Unlike the common law rule that a counteroffer terminates the original offer, under r.49 an offer to settle remains open until it is: (1) withdrawn by the offeror under r.49.04(1); or (2) the time for acceptance expires (i.e., commencement of trial if no expiry date is specified, or the stated expiry date). The making of a competing or counter-offer under r.49 does not automatically terminate the original offer in the same way as at common law. The defendant's $50,000 offer therefore remained open — unless the defendant withdrew it — and the plaintiff's $75,000 counter-offer did not terminate it. The plaintiff may still accept the $50,000 offer provided it has not been withdrawn or expired.
why_A_wrong: The statement that both offers remain open simultaneously is essentially correct in the r.49 context — the original offer is not terminated by the counter-offer unless withdrawn — but the explanation in option B is more precise about the mechanism and is the best answer.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under r.49, a counter-offer does not automatically terminate the original offer; the original offer remains open unless withdrawn by the offeror under r.49.04 or expired.
why_C_wrong: While this accurately states the common law principle, r.49 modifies it. Under the Rules of Civil Procedure regime for settlement offers, a counter-offer does not automatically terminate the original offer as it would at common law.
why_D_wrong: There is no requirement under r.49 that a counter-offer be in the same amount as the original offer. Any written offer to settle served on the other party pursuant to r.49.02 is a valid offer under the rule.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Counteroffer — Effect on Original Offer
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.49; r.49.04

tested_concepts: r.49 regime — counter-offer does not terminate original offer
original offer remains open until withdrawn by offeror under r.49.04
distinction from common law counteroffer rule

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Discontinuance — Right to Discontinue and Costs — r.23.01
fact_pattern: The plaintiff in a contract action commenced the action two years ago. After examinations for discovery, the plaintiff decides it no longer wishes to pursue the claim. The statement of defence was delivered eight months ago. The plaintiff asks whether it can simply discontinue the action without a court order.
call_of_question: Under r.23.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, when may a plaintiff discontinue an action without leave of the court, and what are the automatic cost consequences?

options.A: A plaintiff may discontinue an action at any time without leave of the court or any cost consequences; discontinuance is a unilateral right that does not depend on the stage of proceedings.
options.B: Under r.23.01, a plaintiff may discontinue an action without leave of the court at any time before the close of pleadings; after pleadings are closed, leave is required. Where an action is discontinued without leave, the defendant is entitled to costs of the action.
options.C: A plaintiff may discontinue an action only with the consent of all parties; if the defendant does not consent, the plaintiff must proceed to trial.
options.D: A plaintiff may discontinue an action at any time, but only by obtaining a court order with notice to all parties; no unilateral discontinuance is permitted.

explanation: Under r.23.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a plaintiff may discontinue an action against a defendant without leave of the court at any time before the close of pleadings by serving a notice of discontinuance on all parties and filing it with proof of service. After the close of pleadings — as is the case here, since the statement of defence was delivered eight months ago — leave of the court is required to discontinue. On a motion for leave to discontinue, the court considers terms and conditions that are just, typically including an award of costs to the defendant. Upon discontinuance without leave (before close of pleadings), the defendant is automatically entitled to costs of the action up to the date of the notice of discontinuance.
why_A_wrong: A plaintiff does not have an unconditional right to discontinue at any time without consequences. After the close of pleadings, leave is required. Discontinuance also carries automatic cost consequences — the defendant is entitled to costs.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under r.23.01, discontinuance without leave is available before close of pleadings; after close of pleadings, leave is required; defendant is entitled to costs on discontinuance.
why_C_wrong: Consent of all parties is not required for discontinuance. The plaintiff may discontinue as of right before close of pleadings, or with leave of the court thereafter, without the defendant's consent.
why_D_wrong: Before the close of pleadings, discontinuance is available as of right without any court order or notice to the court beyond filing the notice. A court order is only required after the close of pleadings.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Discontinuance — Right to Discontinue and Costs — r.23.01
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.23.01

tested_concepts: r.23.01 — discontinuance without leave before close of pleadings
leave required after close of pleadings
defendant entitled to costs on discontinuance

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: The plaintiff discontinues an action against the defendant. The plaintiff then discovers that the same claim can be re-framed more effectively. The plaintiff's counsel asks whether the plaintiff can recommence an action on the same claim after discontinuance, and whether the limitation period has been tolled during the period the original action was pending.
call_of_question: Under r.23.05 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what is the effect of discontinuance on a plaintiff's ability to recommence an action on the same claim?

options.A: Discontinuance permanently bars any subsequent action on the same claim; it operates as a res judicata.
options.B: Under r.23.05, a plaintiff who has discontinued an action may recommence it, but only with leave of the court if more than one action has previously been discontinued against the same defendant on the same claim.
options.C: Discontinuance has no effect on limitation periods; the limitation clock stopped when the original action was commenced and recommencement is available at any time.
options.D: Discontinuance does not operate as a bar to subsequent proceedings on the same claim, but the limitation period continues to run during the period the action is pending and is not tolled; a plaintiff who discontinues and recommences must ensure the recommenced action is within the limitation period.

explanation: Under r.23.05 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194) and the Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, discontinuance does not operate as a permanent bar to recommencing an action on the same claim — it is not res judicata. However, under the Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, there is no general provision suspending or tolling the limitation period during the pendency of a proceeding that is subsequently discontinued. The limitation period is generally not tolled or suspended during the period an action is pending; it continues to run. If the plaintiff discontinues and the limitation period has expired before recommencement, the new action will be time-barred. The practical risk of discontinuance is that if significant time has passed, the limitation period may have expired.
why_A_wrong: Discontinuance does not operate as res judicata. There has been no judgment on the merits; res judicata requires a final adjudication on the merits. The plaintiff may recommence, subject to limitation periods.
why_B_wrong: While r.23.05 does address recommencement after multiple discontinuances (requiring leave after more than one discontinuance), the broader and more important point is the limitation period question, which is the critical concern in the scenario. Option D is the most accurate and complete answer.
why_C_wrong: The limitation period is not tolled during the pendency of a discontinued action. The plaintiff cannot assume the limitation clock stopped when the original action was commenced; if the period has run before recommencement, the claim is time-barred.
why_D_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Discontinuance does not bar recommencement, but limitation periods are not tolled; the plaintiff must ensure recommencement is within the limitation period.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Discontinuance — Effect on Limitation Periods
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.23.05; Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B

tested_concepts: discontinuance not res judicata — recommencement permitted
limitation period not tolled during discontinued action
recommencement must be within limitation period

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Settlement Approval for Minors and Mentally Incapable Persons — r.7.08
fact_pattern: The plaintiff is a 14-year-old injured in a motor vehicle accident. The parties have negotiated a settlement of $125,000. The plaintiff's litigation guardian — the plaintiff's parent — has agreed to the settlement on the plaintiff's behalf. The defendant's counsel asks whether the settlement is enforceable without any further steps.
call_of_question: Under r.7.08 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, is a settlement of a claim by a minor enforceable without court approval?

options.A: Yes — a settlement agreed to by a litigation guardian on behalf of a minor is binding and enforceable without any further steps; the litigation guardian's consent is sufficient.
options.B: No — under r.7.08, no settlement of a claim by or against a person under disability (including a minor) is binding unless it is approved by a judge; both parties must bring a motion for approval.
options.C: Court approval is required only if the settlement amount exceeds $100,000; settlements below that threshold are binding without approval.
options.D: Court approval is required only if the litigation guardian requests it; if the litigation guardian is satisfied with the settlement, no approval is needed.

explanation: Under r.7.08 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), no agreement or settlement of a claim by or against a person under disability — including a minor or a mentally incapable person — is binding on that person unless it is approved by a judge. This rule protects vulnerable parties who cannot protect their own interests. The motion for approval is typically brought on notice to the Children's Lawyer (for minors) or the Public Guardian and Trustee (for mentally incapable persons), who may review the settlement and make submissions. The judge considers whether the settlement is in the best interests of the person under disability before approving it. The litigation guardian's agreement alone is not sufficient to make the settlement binding.
why_A_wrong: A litigation guardian's consent alone is insufficient to make a settlement binding on a minor. Court approval under r.7.08 is a mandatory requirement that cannot be waived by the litigation guardian.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under r.7.08, no settlement on behalf of a minor or person under disability is binding without judicial approval; the motion must be brought regardless of the amount.
why_C_wrong: There is no $100,000 threshold below which court approval is not required. Rule 7.08 requires court approval for all settlements involving persons under disability, regardless of the amount.
why_D_wrong: Court approval is mandatory under r.7.08 regardless of whether the litigation guardian is satisfied with the settlement. The rule is designed to protect the person under disability independently of the litigation guardian's views.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Settlement Approval for Minors and Mentally Incapable Persons — r.7.08
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.7.08

tested_concepts: r.7.08 — mandatory court approval for minor/incapable person settlement
litigation guardian's consent alone insufficient
Children's Lawyer notified for minors

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Effect of Acceptance — Staying Proceedings and Consent Judgment
fact_pattern: The plaintiff accepts the defendant's offer to settle for $80,000 by serving written acceptance. The plaintiff then files a notice of acceptance with the court and asks the registrar to enter a judgment for $80,000 against the defendant. The defendant objects, arguing the plaintiff cannot enter judgment unilaterally after acceptance.
call_of_question: Under r.49.09 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what are the consequences of an accepted offer to settle for the underlying action, and how is the settlement enforced?

options.A: Acceptance of an offer to settle automatically converts the accepted offer into a judgment of the court, which may be enforced immediately without further steps.
options.B: Under r.49.09, where an offer to settle is accepted, the court may stay the action or make such other orders as are just; if the defendant fails to comply with the terms of the accepted offer, the plaintiff may move to enforce the settlement agreement, obtain a consent judgment, or have the action dismissed.
options.C: Acceptance of an offer to settle operates as a final dismissal of the action; the plaintiff cannot take any further steps in the action and must commence a new action if the defendant fails to pay.
options.D: After acceptance, the plaintiff may immediately enter a judgment by registrar for the amount of the offer without any further motion or consent from the defendant.

explanation: Under r.49.09 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), upon acceptance of an offer to settle, the court may stay the proceeding in whole or in part on such terms as are just, or make such other order as is just. The accepted offer creates a binding contractual settlement, but it does not automatically convert into a judgment. If the defendant fails to comply with the terms of the settlement (e.g., fails to pay the agreed amount), the plaintiff may move to enforce the settlement by seeking a consent judgment, bringing a motion on the settlement agreement, or having the action dismissed on the agreed terms. The plaintiff cannot unilaterally enter a judgment without a court order or consent.
why_A_wrong: An accepted offer does not automatically become a court judgment. The settlement is a binding contract, but enforcement requires further steps if the defendant fails to comply — typically a motion for a consent judgment.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under r.49.09, acceptance creates a binding settlement; the court may stay proceedings or make just orders; if the defendant defaults the plaintiff may move to enforce.
why_C_wrong: Acceptance does not operate as an automatic final dismissal of the action. The action continues in a stayed or suspended state until the settlement is performed; if the defendant fails to comply, the plaintiff has remedies within the action.
why_D_wrong: The plaintiff cannot enter a registrar's judgment unilaterally based solely on acceptance of an offer to settle. A court order or consent is required to convert the accepted offer into an enforceable judgment.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Effect of Acceptance — Staying Proceedings and Consent Judgment
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.49.09

tested_concepts: r.49.09 — court may stay action after acceptance
accepted offer is binding contract not automatic judgment
if defendant defaults — motion to enforce or consent judgment

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: As Favourable As — Meaning Under r.49.10
fact_pattern: The plaintiff served an offer to settle for $100,000 inclusive of costs. At trial, the plaintiff is awarded $100,000 in damages plus $18,000 in costs for a total judgment package of $118,000. The defendant argues that because the judgment is $100,000 — the same amount as the offer — it is 'as favourable as' the offer, and the defendant should not have to pay substantial indemnity costs after the offer date.
call_of_question: Under r.49.10(2), when the plaintiff's offer is for $100,000 'inclusive of costs' and the plaintiff recovers $100,000 in damages plus costs, is the judgment 'as favourable as' the offer?

options.A: Yes — the damage award of $100,000 is exactly equal to the offer amount; the r.49.10(2) trigger is met only on damages, and the offer is considered 'as favourable as' the judgment.
options.B: No — where the plaintiff's offer is inclusive of costs, the comparison for r.49.10(2) purposes must include both the damages and costs awarded; a judgment of $100,000 in damages plus separately awarded costs of $18,000 represents a total recovery exceeding the $100,000 inclusive-of-costs offer, so the plaintiff has beaten the offer and r.49.10(2) applies.
options.C: An offer expressed 'inclusive of costs' cannot attract r.49.10 cost consequences at all; only offers that do not reference costs trigger r.49.10.
options.D: The comparison under r.49.10(2) excludes costs entirely; only the damages component of the judgment is compared to the offer.

explanation: Under r.49.10(2) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the relevant comparison is between the terms of the offer and the outcome achieved at trial, taken as a whole. Where the plaintiff's offer is expressed 'inclusive of costs,' the comparison must take into account both the damages and costs components. A judgment of $100,000 in damages plus $18,000 in separately awarded costs represents a total recovery of $118,000. This exceeds the plaintiff's $100,000 inclusive-of-costs offer, meaning the plaintiff has beaten its own offer. Rule 49.10(2) therefore applies, entitling the plaintiff to partial indemnity costs to the date of the offer and substantial indemnity costs from the date of the offer. The defendant cannot avoid the r.49.10(2) consequences by comparing only the damages award to the inclusive-of-costs offer.
why_A_wrong: The comparison under r.49.10(2) where the offer is expressed inclusive of costs must include both the damages and costs awarded at trial. Comparing only the damages award of $100,000 to an inclusive-of-costs offer of $100,000 ignores the separately awarded costs and understates the plaintiff's recovery.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Where the offer is inclusive of costs, the total judgment (damages plus costs) is compared to the offer; $118,000 > $100,000 inclusive of costs, so the plaintiff has beaten the offer and r.49.10(2) applies.
why_C_wrong: Offers inclusive of costs are valid under r.49 and can attract cost consequences under r.49.10. The inclusive-of-costs formulation affects how the comparison is made but does not exclude the offer from the r.49.10 regime.
why_D_wrong: The comparison under r.49.10 cannot exclude costs where the offer expressly states it is inclusive of costs. The whole point of an inclusive-of-costs offer is that costs are folded into the settlement amount; the comparison must therefore be to the total recovery.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: As Favourable As — Meaning Under r.49.10
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.49.10(2)

tested_concepts: inclusive of costs offer — total recovery compared
r.49.10(2) — damages plus separately awarded costs vs inclusive offer
plaintiff beats offer where total recovery exceeds inclusive amount
